story.title: Maya and the Moon Numbers

pages.text: Maya loved school — she loved reading, she loved painting, and she especially loved the way numbers clicked together like puzzle pieces. But one rainy Tuesday, something happened that stuck to her like mud on her favorite sneakers. The class was lining up for math groups, and a boy named Derek said, loud enough for everyone to hear, 'Girls aren't really good at math. My brother told me so.' A few kids laughed. Maya didn't laugh. She looked down at her worksheet covered in gold stars and suddenly felt unsure, like the stars didn't count anymore. That night, she sat at the kitchen table and pushed her math homework away. 'Maybe I should just stick to drawing,' she whispered to her cat, Pepper, who blinked slowly and said nothing at all.
pages.illustration_description: A cozy kitchen scene at dusk. Maya, a six-year-old girl with dark brown skin, big curious brown eyes, and her hair in two afro puffs with yellow ribbons, sits at a wooden kitchen table. She's wearing a purple t-shirt with a small star on it and jeans. Her math homework is pushed to the side, and a fluffy gray cat named Pepper sits on the chair next to her. Gold star stickers are visible on the worksheet. Rain streaks the window behind her. The mood is quiet and a little sad, with warm yellow kitchen light contrasting the blue-gray rain outside.

pages.text: The next morning, Maya's class went to the library for free reading time. She wandered past the fairy tale shelf, past the dinosaur books, past the chapter books she wasn't quite ready for yet. Then something caught her eye — a book had fallen open on the floor near the back corner, as if it had been waiting for her. On the page was a photograph of a woman sitting at a desk covered in numbers, with a pencil in her hand and the biggest, most confident smile Maya had ever seen. Behind the woman was a picture of a rocket ship, mid-launch, blazing with golden fire. 'She doesn't look like someone who thinks girls can't do math,' Maya murmured. She picked up the book and felt a tingle in her fingers, like the beginning of something wonderful.
pages.illustration_description: A warm, sunlit school library with tall wooden shelves filled with colorful books. Maya is crouching down near a back corner, reaching for an open book on the floor. A beam of golden sunlight falls across the open pages like a spotlight. On the visible page is a black-and-white photograph of Katherine Johnson — an African American woman with glasses and a warm smile, sitting at a desk full of papers and equations. A rocket illustration glows on the opposite page. Maya's eyes are wide with curiosity. Her purple star shirt and yellow hair ribbons are visible. The mood is magical and full of quiet wonder, with dust motes floating in the light.

pages.text: Maya curled up in a beanbag chair and began to read, and the library seemed to grow quieter around her, as if the whole room were listening too. The woman's name was Katherine Johnson, and she had worked for NASA — the people who send rockets into space. Katherine didn't just watch the rockets fly; she was the one who figured out where they needed to go, using math so careful and so perfect that it could carry people to the moon and back. Then Maya read the part that made her heart beat fast: when the giant computers made a mistake, everyone in the room turned to Katherine. 'If she says the numbers are right,' the astronaut said, 'then I'm ready to fly.' Maya read that sentence three times. A real astronaut, about to blast into the dark unknown of space, trusted this woman's math more than a machine. Maya hugged the book to her chest and whispered, 'She did it with numbers — just like the ones on my worksheet.'
pages.illustration_description: Maya is nestled in a large red beanbag chair, holding the open book close to her face, completely absorbed. Around her, the library has taken on a dreamy, magical quality — the edges of the shelves are slightly blurred and soft. Above Maya's head, like an imagination cloud, a vivid scene unfolds: a NASA control room from the 1960s with men in white shirts and ties all turning to look at Katherine Johnson, who stands confidently at a chalkboard covered in trajectory equations. A small rocket model sits on a nearby desk, and through a large window, a real rocket is visible on the launch pad. Katherine is drawn with warm, glowing lines. Stars and numbers gently float in the air between the imagined scene and Maya. The mood is awe-filled and magical.

pages.text: The book told Maya Katherine's whole story, and it read like the most incredible fairy tale she'd ever found. When Katherine was young, she loved counting everything — the steps to church, the stars in the sky, the dishes on the table. She was so brilliant that she started high school when she was only ten years old. But the world was not always kind to her; there were people who said she didn't belong because she was a woman, and because she was Black. Doors were closed in her face, but Katherine walked through every single one of them anyway. She joined NASA and filled chalkboards with swirling equations that mapped paths through the stars. Katherine's math was so precise that astronauts trusted her calculations with their lives — even over the computers. She helped send the first Americans into orbit and helped guide Apollo 11 all the way to the moon. One woman, one pencil, and numbers that stretched from the Earth to the glowing lunar surface — that was all it took to change history.
pages.illustration_description: A full-page, fairy-tale-style illustration of Katherine Johnson's journey. The page is split into flowing, dreamlike panels connected by swirling mathematical equations that look like golden ribbons. Top left: a young Katherine as a little girl counting bright stars in a deep blue sky. Center: a teenage Katherine at a chalkboard in a classroom, writing equations while older students look on in surprise. Bottom center: an adult Katherine at NASA, drawing elegant curved trajectory lines on a giant chalkboard, wearing a neat dress and glasses, looking powerful and serene. Right side: a magnificent Saturn V rocket launches in a blaze of orange and gold, its trajectory line connecting back to Katherine's chalk marks. The moon glows silver at the top of the page. The overall palette is deep blues, golds, silvers, and warm oranges. The mood is epic, triumphant, and magical — like a legend being told.

pages.text: When Maya got home that afternoon, she didn't push her math homework away. She pulled it close. She picked up her pencil the way she imagined Katherine once did — with purpose, like it was a key that could unlock the universe. The problems weren't easy, but Maya worked through them one by one, and each time she got an answer right, she felt that tingle in her fingers again. At dinner, her mom asked how school was, and Maya said, 'Did you know that a woman's math sent people to the moon? Her numbers were better than the computers.' Her mom smiled wide. The next day at school, when Derek said something about girls and math again, Maya didn't feel mud on her sneakers. She felt rocket fuel. 'Katherine Johnson flew astronauts to the moon with her math,' Maya said simply. 'And I'm going to see what my numbers can do.' Then she opened her workbook, and began.
pages.illustration_description: A bright, hopeful scene split into two moments. The main image shows Maya back at her kitchen table, but everything has changed — she's leaning forward with a big, determined smile, pencil in hand, working through her math homework with energy. Pepper the cat watches from the chair with wide, impressed eyes. Gold stars from her previous worksheets are pinned to the wall behind her, and a small printout of Katherine Johnson's photo is now taped next to them. Through the kitchen window, a night sky full of stars and a bright moon is visible. In the bottom corner, a smaller vignette shows Maya at school the next day, standing tall at her desk with her workbook open, looking confident and joyful, with light seeming to radiate around her. Her purple star shirt and yellow ribbons are vivid. The overall mood is warm, triumphant, and full of possibility.

story.real_woman_achievement: Katherine Johnson calculated the flight trajectories for NASA's first human spaceflights, including Apollo 11's historic trip to the moon, and her math was so trusted that astronauts relied on her calculations over the computers.
discussion_prompts: Has anyone ever told you that you couldn't do something? How did it make you feel, and what do you think now after hearing Maya's story?
Why do you think the astronaut trusted Katherine's math even more than the giant computers?
What is something you love doing that you want to get even better at, just like Katherine got better and better at math?
story.activity_suggestion: Moon Trajectory Math Game: Using a piece of paper, draw the Earth on one side and the Moon on the other. Create a simple connect-the-dots path between them using numbered dots (in counting or addition sequence). As your child solves each small math problem, they draw the next part of the line — 'calculating' the rocket's trajectory just like Katherine Johnson did. When they finish, they've guided their rocket to the moon with their own math!

classification.stereotype_essence: The false belief that girls are inherently less capable of understanding, enjoying, or excelling in mathematics due to their gender.

age_strategy.young: Use playful counting games, shape puzzles, and stories featuring girl characters who love numbers to normalize math as something everyone enjoys. At this age, focus on making math feel fun and natural rather than directly addressing the stereotype.
age_strategy.middle: Introduce real-world women mathematicians and scientists as role models (e.g., Katherine Johnson, Maryam Mirzakhani) and emphasize that math ability comes from practice and curiosity, not gender. Encourage hands-on math activities where girls experience success firsthand.
age_strategy.older: Directly address the stereotype with evidence and data showing girls perform equally well in math globally. Discuss how societal expectations—not ability—create confidence gaps. Empower critical thinking about where these messages come from and encourage girls to claim their mathematical identity proudly.

real_woman.name: Katherine Johnson
real_woman.achievement: Calculated the flight trajectories for NASA's first human spaceflights, including Apollo 11's trip to the moon

issues: The stereotype source (Derek) is a boy, which could subtly reinforce a 'boys vs. girls' framing rather than treating the stereotype as a broader societal issue. However, this is minor and realistic for the age group.

Maya's pre-existing love of math and gold stars slightly reduces the narrative tension — a child who already excels may not fully represent the experience of girls who struggle with math confidence without prior success.
strengths: The story never reinforces the target stereotype; Maya's doubt is portrayed as externally imposed and temporary, not as evidence of any truth to the claim.
No new stereotypes are introduced — Maya is not described by appearance, and her identity is built around curiosity, emotion, and intellectual agency.
Katherine Johnson's story is woven in naturally through a library discovery, feeling organic rather than didactic or like a lecture.

Katherine Johnson is presented with full dimensionality: her childhood love of counting, the systemic barriers she faced (gender and race), and her extraordinary achievements are all included without reducing her to a single trait.
The intersectionality of Katherine Johnson's experience (woman and Black) is acknowledged honestly and age-appropriately without being glossed over or overexplained.
The language is vivid, lyrical, and age-appropriate — metaphors like 'mud on her sneakers' and 'rocket fuel' are concrete and emotionally resonant for young readers.
The ending is empowering but realistic: Maya doesn't suddenly become a genius; she commits to trying, which models growth mindset rather than magical transformation.
Maya's response to Derek at the end is assertive without being aggressive or mean — she leads with facts and personal resolve rather than shaming him.
The cat Pepper adds warmth and realism without distracting from the narrative.
The tone avoids preachiness throughout; the moral emerges from story and character rather than from explicit moralizing.
qa_result.suggestion: Consider briefly acknowledging that boys and girls alike can struggle with math confidence, or give Derek a small moment of curiosity or reconsideration, to avoid a purely adversarial boy-vs-girl dynamic and model that stereotypes harm everyone.
